features: pUC ori | ori | ColE1 ori | pMB1 ori
notes: pUC19-derived ori (mutated pMB1) gives ~500-700 copies/cell due to loss of rop regulation. Often annotated just as 'ori'. Module is typically 600-1000 bp.
Presence of rop/rom protein argues for low-copy pBR322-style ori, not high-copy pUC.
ColE1-family replication is regulated by antisense RNA I/RNA II. Rarely annotated but diagnostic when present.
pSC101 ori — strict low-copy (~5 copies). Incompatibility group IncW-like. ~1.5 kb module including repA.
p15A ori (from pACYC184) — ~10-12 copies. Compatible with pUC/ColE1 oris for co-transformation.
CloDF13 — ~20-40 copies. Compatible with ColE1 and p15A; used in triple-plasmid systems.
ColA and RSF are additional E. coli origins in orthogonal compatibility groups (Duet vector family).
pSC101 RepA (or its temperature-sensitive variant) is the trans-acting factor required for pSC101 replication.
rop/rom protein reduces ColE1-family copy number; presence indicates low-copy variant.
R6Kγ ori requires the π protein (pir gene) in trans — plasmid only replicates in pir+ host strains (e.g. BW25141, EC100D pir+). Used for suicide vectors and conditional propagation.
If pir is on the same plasmid it is self-sufficient; more commonly pir is host-encoded.
pBBR1 replicon — replicates in many Gram-negative bacteria (Rhizobium, Agrobacterium, Brucella, Bordetella). Small (~2 kb), mobilizable.

features: RSF1010 oriV + RSF1010 RepA + RSF1010 RepB + RSF1010 RepC
notes: RSF1010 IncQ replicon — the broadest host range known, replicates in virtually all Gram-negatives and some Gram-positives.
pVS1 replicon from Pseudomonas — the standard Agrobacterium ori in binary vectors (pBIN, pCAMBIA series). Stable in Agrobacterium but not E. coli.
pRO1600 replicon — Pseudomonas aeruginosa origin used in shuttle vectors.
pSa ori — broad host range, IncW incompatibility group.
RK2/RP4 replicon — IncP broad-host-range. trfA protein is essential in trans for oriV function. Used for bacterial conjugation and broad host expression.
Agrobacterium rhizogenes Ri plasmid replicator; alternative to pVS1 in some plant binary vectors.
Mini-F replicon: repE initiates replication, sopABC is the partition system that ensures single-copy stability. ~5-6 kb module.
ori2 is the actual origin of mini-F replication, bound by RepE. Supporting evidence.
incC incompatibility determinant — part of the F replicon regulatory region.
f1/M13 intergenic region (IG) — the origin of rolling-circle replication. Direction determines which strand is packaged. ~450 bp. Requires helper phage (M13K07, VCS-M13) for pIII protein in trans.
gene II protein nicks the (+) strand to initiate rolling-circle replication. If present on the plasmid itself, the plasmid is self-sufficient for ssDNA production.

features: M13 gene V | M13 gene X
notes: gene V (ssDNA binding) and gene X (rolling-circle) proteins; supporting for M13-based ssDNA production.
N15 linear plasmid origin — used in linear BAC systems with telN protelomerase to generate linear closed-end plasmids.
S. cerevisiae 2-micron plasmid origin — high-copy (~20-40 copies), stable via FLP/FRT amplification system. Used in high-expression yeast vectors (YEp-series).
FLP recombinase is part of the 2μ copy number amplification machinery. Often present in natural 2μ but stripped from minimal yeast shuttles.
CEN (centromere) + ARS (autonomously replicating sequence) — gives low-copy (1-2 copies) stable mitotic inheritance. Used in YCp-series vectors for stoichiometric expression studies.
ARS1 alone — higher copy but unstable without CEN; used in YRp-series vectors which are rapidly lost without selection.
S. pombe ars1; used in pREP/pDUAL vectors for fission yeast.
Autonomously maintained in Aspergillus/Pichia; enables episomal propagation in filamentous fungi and some yeasts.

SV40 promoter immediately followed by SV40 ori is the SV40 replication+early expression module. The ori overlaps with the promoter TATA box region. In T-antigen-expressing cells (COS-1, COS-7) the plasmid replicates to high copy number.
SV40 ori alone (~180 bp) is sufficient for replication in T-antigen-expressing cells; weight is lower because it's often included without intent to replicate (as a marker of vector lineage).

features: large T antigen | small t antigen
notes: If large T antigen is encoded on the same plasmid (not just the host), the plasmid is self-sufficient for SV40 replication in any cell type. Rare but diagnostic when present.
EBV oriP paired with EBNA1 gives stable episomal maintenance in human cells (1-10 copies/cell) over many divisions. Used in pCEP4, pREP-series, and iPSC reprogramming vectors.
oriP alone requires EBNA1 in trans (host-provided or separate plasmid). mini-oriP lacks the family-of-repeats enhancer and is smaller but still functional with EBNA1.

features: BK virus ori | BKV ori | polyoma ori
notes: BK polyomavirus origin — alternative to SV40 ori, used in some human-cell episomal systems. Not present in the reference CSV; rule included for completeness.

features: bla promoter | AmpR promoter | cat promoter
notes: Constitutive promoters native to the resistance gene (beta-lactamase, chloramphenicol acetyltransferase). Often unannotated when the marker is the default, but when present they clearly mark the module.
β-lactamase — confers ampicillin/carbenicillin resistance. The default E. coli selection marker. ~860 bp CDS.
The native AmpR promoter is small and unregulated; typically directly upstream of the bla ORF.
Neomycin phosphotransferase (aph(3')-II or aph(3')-Ia) — kanamycin resistance in bacteria AND G418/neomycin resistance in eukaryotes when driven by a mammalian promoter. Dual-use marker.
Chloramphenicol acetyltransferase — chloramphenicol resistance. Small (~660 bp). Common in BACs and low-copy vectors.
Tetracycline efflux pump — tetracycline resistance. Large module (~1.2 kb) because tetA is a membrane protein.
Streptomycin / spectinomycin resistance (aadA). Common in plant and Agrobacterium vectors.
Gentamicin / apramycin resistance — aminoglycoside acetyltransferase variants. Used in broad-host-range vectors.
Hygromycin phosphotransferase — works in bacteria, plants, and mammalian cells depending on promoter.
Sh ble — zeocin/bleomycin/phleomycin resistance. Works across bacteria, yeast, plants, and mammalian cells with appropriate promoters — a true universal marker.
Nourseothricin / bialaphos resistance — used primarily in yeast and fungi.
Many bacterial selection cassettes lack explicit terminators and rely on read-through into plasmid backbone — terminator absence is not a disqualifier.
URA3 complements ura3- hosts; module includes native promoter and terminator. Can be counter-selected with 5-FOA.
LEU2 complements leu2- hosts.
HIS3 for S. cerevisiae histidine auxotrophy. HIS3MX6 is the heterologous-promoter cassette version used for tagging.
TRP1 complements trp1- hosts.

features: [LYS2 promoter] + LYS2 | [ADE2 promoter] + ADE2 | [MET17 promoter] + MET17
notes: Other auxotrophic markers. ADE2 gives a color phenotype (red on limited adenine) useful for colony screening.

features: S. pombe his3 | S. pombe his5 | S. pombe ura4
notes: S. pombe-specific auxotrophic markers.
Pichia pastoris auxotrophic markers.

features: PGK promoter | SV40 promoter | TK promoter | EF-1α core promoter | EM7 promoter
notes: Weak constitutive promoters are typical for mammalian selection cassettes (strong promoters would overwhelm the payload). EM7 is special: a synthetic bacterial promoter often placed between a mammalian promoter and the marker so the same cassette works in both hosts.
Puromycin N-acetyltransferase (pac) — puromycin resistance. Fast selection (2-4 days). Very common.
Same CDS as bacterial KanR but driven by a mammalian promoter — gives G418 resistance in mammalian cells. The context (promoter identity) disambiguates bacterial vs. mammalian selection.
Hygromycin resistance — slower selection than puro but usable alongside puro for dual selection.
Blasticidin S deaminase — blasticidin resistance. Small CDS (~400 bp), fast selection.
Zeocin resistance — works in mammalian cells when driven by a mammalian promoter or bifunctional cassette.

features: SV40 poly(A) signal | PGK poly(A) signal | HSV TK poly(A) signal | bGH poly(A) signal
notes: Short polyA signals are standard in selection cassettes; weaker than payload-grade polyAs are often used.

EM7 is a synthetic constitutive E. coli promoter. When present between a mammalian promoter and a selection marker, it creates a bifunctional cassette selectable in both E. coli and mammalian cells — diagnostic of lentiviral, AAV, and Gateway destination vectors.
CcdB DNA gyrase poison — kills standard E. coli; propagated only in ccdB-resistant strains (DB3.1, ccdB Survival). Used in Gateway destination vectors to select against empty vector.
Bacillus subtilis levansucrase — sucrose sensitivity in Gram-negatives. Classic counter-selection for allelic exchange in bacterial genetics.
Diphtheria toxin A chain — negative selection in mammalian cells; used in knock-in targeting vectors to kill random integrants.
Bacillus amyloliquefaciens RNase — toxic without its inhibitor (barstar). Used for counter-selection in plants and bacteria.
E. coli cytosine deaminase — converts 5-fluorocytosine to 5-fluorouracil. Prodrug-based negative selection in mammalian cells.
URA3 is a positive auxotrophic marker but also a counter-selection marker via 5-FOA. Context-dependent — the same CDS serves both purposes.
Origin of transfer (nic site) — minimum cis-requirement for conjugative mobilization. Small (~250 bp). Needs tra genes in trans from a helper plasmid.
Relaxase genes; if present on the same plasmid it is self-mobilizable rather than merely mobilizable.
loxP site (34 bp) — the standard Cre recognition site. Two loxP sites in the same orientation flanking cargo = floxed/excisable cassette. Two in inverted orientation = invertible cassette.
The second loxP completes the floxed cassette. Orientation relative to the 5' loxP determines mechanism: direct repeat → excision; inverted → inversion (flip-flop).

features: lox2272 | loxN | lox5171 | loxP511 | loxH | loxJTZ17 | loxKR3
notes: Variant lox sites are mutually incompatible with wild-type loxP — used for multi-site recombination schemes (Brainbow, RMCE). Two sites of the same variant recombine but cross-variant recombination is disfavored.
Symmetric loxP — recombination is orientation-independent. Used in synthetic genomics (SCRaMbLE).
lox66 and lox71 recombine to form a double-mutant lox72 that is a poor Cre substrate — one-way recombination for stable cassette integration.
FLP recognition target (48 bp minimal) — yeast S. cerevisiae-derived recombinase site. Two direct repeats → excision. Also used for RMCE with mutant FRTs.
Second FRT completes the flippable cassette.

Gateway site 1 — the 5' boundary of a Gateway cassette. Site identity indicates stage: attB = PCR product, attL = entry clone, attR = destination vector, attP = donor vector. Pairs with site 2 at the 3' end.
Gateway site 2 — the 3' boundary. Must match the 5' site type (L-L, R-R, B-B, or P-P) for a coherent clone.
The canonical Gateway destination vector cassette: attR1 → ccdB (counter-selection) → CmR (to maintain ccdB plasmid in DB3.1) → attR2. Diagnostic of a destination vector awaiting LR reaction.

features: attB3 | attB4 | attB5 | attB6 | attL3 | attL4 | attL5 | attL6 | attR3 | attR4 | attR5 | attR6 | attP3 | attP4 | attP5 | attP6
notes: Multisite Gateway (MultiSite Gateway Pro) uses 3-6 orthogonal att pairs for multi-fragment assembly.

features: λ attP | λ attB | phage Φ80 attP | phage φ21 attP | phage φC31 attP | HK022 attP | phage 186 attP
notes: Phage integrase attachment sites — used for single-copy integration into genomic attB landing pads in bacteria and (for φC31) mammalian cells.

features: λ integrase | phage φ80 integrase | HK022 integrase | phage φ21 integrase | phage φC31 integrase | phage 186 integrase
notes: The integrase enzyme itself; if encoded on the same plasmid, the construct is self-sufficient for integration.

Multiple cloning site — a compact stretch of unique restriction sites for traditional cloning. Often between a promoter and terminator in expression vectors, or flanked by primer binding sites in cloning vectors.

α-complementation fragment of β-galactosidase — enables blue/white screening when the MCS is within lacZα and the host is lacZΔM15 (DH5α, XL1-Blue). Disruption of lacZα by an insert gives a white colony on X-gal.

features: M13 fwd | M13 rev | M13 pUC fwd primer | M13 pUC rev primer | M13 forward20 primer | M13 reverse primer | KS primer | SK primer | T7 Terminal primer | BGH rev primer | CMV fwd primer | pBAD fwd primer | pBAD rev primer | Sp6 primer | pGEX 3 primer
notes: Standard sequencing primer binding sites flanking the MCS — allow Sanger sequencing of inserts without custom primers. Their presence flanking an MCS is nearly diagnostic of a cloning vector.

features: T7 promoter + MCS + T3 promoter | T7 promoter + MCS + SP6 promoter
notes: Dual phage promoters flanking an MCS — enables in vitro transcription of either strand. Diagnostic of pBluescript, pGEM, pCR-Blunt, and similar cloning/IVT vectors.

Chicken β-globin HS4 (cHS4) insulator — blocks enhancer-promoter interactions across module boundaries and protects against position-effect silencing. Often placed just inside LTRs in lentiviral vectors.
Second insulator completing the insulated block.
Drosophila gypsy insulator — used in insect transgenic systems and some mammalian contexts.

Ubiquitous chromatin opening element — keeps the adjacent promoter in an open chromatin state, resistant to silencing. Used in CHO cell line stable expression. Not in the reference CSV; rule included for completeness.

Sleeping Beauty left inverted repeat/direct repeat (IR/DR). SB100X transposase recognizes these for cut-and-paste integration at TA dinucleotides. Not in the reference CSV as such; rule assumes custom annotation.
SB right ITR/DR completing the transposon.
SB100X hyperactive transposase — can be on the same plasmid (single-plasmid system) or co-delivered (two-plasmid).

features: PB ITR (5') | PiggyBac ITR (5') | PB 5' TRD
notes: PiggyBac 5' terminal repeat domain. PB transposase integrates at TTAA sites without leaving a footprint after excision (seamless). Not explicitly in the reference CSV.

features: PB ITR (3') | PiggyBac ITR (3') | PB 3' TRD
notes: PiggyBac 3' TRD. Cargo between the 5' and 3' TRDs is mobilized into TTAA target sites.
Tn5 Mosaic End — recognized by Tn5 transposase for in vitro tagmentation (Nextera) or in vivo insertion. Two MEs in inverted orientation define the transposable element.
Tn7 left end — Tn7 transposase integrates the flanked cargo into the attTn7 site in the E. coli chromosome (or DH10Bac bacmid in Bac-to-Bac).
Tn7 right end. Tn7L/Tn7R flanking the payload is the defining signature of a Bac-to-Bac donor plasmid.
Drosophila P element 5' inverted repeat — used for germ-line transformation in Drosophila.
P element 3' inverted repeat.

features: tTA | tTA-Advanced | rtTA | rtTA-Advanced | rtTA3 | Tet-On® 3G
notes: tTA = Tet-Off (active without doxycycline); rtTA = Tet-On (active with dox). These are VP16-TetR fusions that activate TRE-containing promoters. The regulator is typically on a separate cassette from the TRE-driven payload.
Wild-type TetR (no VP16 fusion) — represses tet operator-containing promoters in the absence of dox. Used in 'tet-off' repression systems without transactivation.
Tet transcriptional silencer — TetR-KRAB fusion for active repression in the absence of dox.

features: GAL4 | GAL4 DNA binding domain + GAL4 activation domain
notes: Gal4 or split Gal4 — activates UAS-containing promoters. Workhorse of Drosophila genetics (Gal4/UAS binary system) and yeast two-hybrid.
Upstream Activation Sequence — Gal4 binding sites driving a responsive promoter.
Gal80 represses Gal4; temperature-sensitive Gal80ts enables thermal control of Gal4/UAS expression in Drosophila.
LexA DNA binding domain — orthogonal to Gal4 for dual-system expression control. Pairs with lexA operator-containing promoters.
OsTIR1 (F-box protein) hijacks the host SCF ubiquitin ligase to target AID-tagged proteins for degradation in the presence of auxin. Fast, reversible protein depletion in any eukaryote.
Chemically inducible dimerization with rapamycin or rapalogs. FKBP and FRB are typically fused to different proteins to bring them together.

features: Cas9 | dCas9 | SpCas9-HF1 | SpCas9-HF2 | SpCas9-HF4 | eSpCas9(1.1) | HypaCas9 | xCas9-3.6 | xCas9-3.7 | dxCas9(3.7) | evoCas9 | pcoCas9 | Cas9(D10A) | Cas9(H840A) | Cas9 VQR | Cas9 VRER | SaCas9 | St1Cas9 | St1dCas9 | NmCas9 | NmdCas9 | CjCas9 | ScCas9
notes: Cas9 variants — the nuclease/nickase/dead effector. Variant identity indicates use case: dCas9 for CRISPRi/a/base editing, HF/esp/hypa for high-fidelity, xCas9/Cas9-VQR/VRER for expanded PAMs.

features: LbCpf1 | AsCpf1 | Cas12 | LwCas13a | dLwCas13a | LshC2c2 | RfxCas13d
notes: Alternative CRISPR effectors: Cas12 (Cpf1) for T-rich PAMs and staggered cuts; Cas13 for RNA targeting.

features: dCas9 + (VP64 | VP48 | VP96 | VP160 | p65 | Rta AD | p300 core | KRAB | KRAB-A | Mxi1 | DNMT3A | TET1 | MeCP2)
notes: dCas9 fused to an effector domain = CRISPRa (VP64/p65/Rta/p300), CRISPRi (KRAB/Mxi1), or epigenome editing (DNMT3A, TET1, MeCP2). Fusion architecture is diagnostic.

features: Cas9(D10A) + (APOBEC-1 | APOBEC3A | PmCDA1 | TadA | TadA*(7.10) | TadA*(7.8) | TadA*(7.9) | TadA*(6.3)) + [UGI]
notes: Base editors: CBE = Cas9n + cytidine deaminase (APOBEC/PmCDA1) + UGI; ABE = Cas9n + TadA/TadA*. The specific combination is diagnostic of the editor class.
